title: Class Methods 🎩
description: Methods that belong strictly to the Blueprint, not the Object.

slides.title: Instance vs Class 🆚
slides.content:
So far, all the methods we wrote were **Instance Methods**. They belonged to the *individual object* (like `fido`).

But sometimes, you want a method to belong strictly to the *blueprint* itself (the Class).

These are called **Class Methods**.

slides.title: The `self` Keyword 🪞
slides.content:
To define a Class Method, you explicitly place the keyword `self.` directly in front of the method name when defining it.

`self` literally means "me, the Class Blueprint, not my future objects!"

```ruby
class Dog
  def self.species
    puts "Canis lupus familiaris"
  end
end
```

slides.title: Calling the Blueprint
slides.content:
Because the `species` method belongs to the Class blueprint, you call it directly ON the Class itself: `Dog.species`.

If you try to call it on an exact instance (like `fido`), it will crash because Fido doesn't own that method!
slides.codeSnippet:
class MathTools
  # A Class Method
  def self.add(a, b)
    a + b
  end
end

# We never created a .new object!
# We just called the built-in Class method directly.
puts MathTools.add(10, 5)

quizData.question: Which prefix physically glues a method securely to the Class blueprint rather than to its future Instances?
quizData.explanation: If the method inside a Class starts with `self.`, it becomes a Class Method. Otherwise, it is a standard Instance Method.

slides.title: When to use them? 🤷‍♂️
slides.content:
You've been using Class Methods for weeks! The most famous Class Method is actually `.new`.

You don't call `.new` on `fido`. You call it on the `Dog` class itself to generate an object.

You use Class methods for utility functions or "factory/generation" logic that doesn't rely on specific object traits like `@name`.

quizData.question: Can a Class Method seamlessly access an object's `@name` instance variable?

options.text: No! A Class method belongs to the blueprint, so it knows absolutely nothing about the individual individual data of the 100 dogs you've built.

quizData.explanation: Variables starting with exactly one `@` are INSTANCE variables. Class methods are blind to them.
